text: Section 6 of Bedtime Stories This is the Libra box recording. All Libra box recordings are in the public domain. For more information or to volunteer, please visit Librabox.org. Read by Greg Giordano. Bedtime Stories by Anonymous. When the old woman moved from the shoe, there was once a little goblin woman who had six sons. They were very ugly, little boys with brown, whizzing faces, and eyes as black as slow as they had very big heads. Though they were so fat, their legs were so thin, you wouldn't believe. Now the little goblin woman thought the world of her six little sons, and she had given them all pretty far names. Slower, butch-up, soirl, bluebell, cow slip, and dandelion. The little goblin woman was very poor, but the way she managed was something wonderful. She and her little family lived in a hollow tree with a little garden rounded. There were three floors inside, and the little string ladder connected the rooms. The first floor was a kitchen, who was ever so clean and cozy. Then it was here that the family had meals and sat in the cold winter evenings, round a little twig fire, listening to the wind and rain outside. The next floor was a bedroom, and the top floor was the drawing room. Its walls were decorated with colored leaves, and it was here that visitors were received. Well, one cold, rainy evening, the whole family was sitting round the kitchen fire, drinking broth. When the little goblin woman said, "'Dandelion, it's your turn to fetch the water tonight, dearie.'" "'Yes, Mom,' said Dandelion, and taking his last drink of broth, he ran to wash his acorn cup and put it away. "'You do make lovely broth, Mom,' said Clover, smacking his lips. "'Not half!' cried the door. With the other five little goblins altogether. "'That's right,' said the little goblin woman. "'Goodbye, Dandelion, lovey, don't be gone all night. "'Goodbye, Mom,' cried Dandelion, nearly hugging the little woman's head off, and away he ran, with the wooden bucket over his head. "'He had not gone far through the forest, near her to sound of crying, so he took the bucket from over his head and looked about. And it was, that he saw a great big shoe standing in the puddle, and it was from this shoe that the sound of crying came. Dandelion crept nearer, and, looking through a little hole in the heel, was just in time to see an old woman smacking a weak girl about his own size. "'Hey,' cried Dandelion, "'stop that!' in her astonishment. The old woman stopped, and a little girl scuttled into the toe of the shoe. May I come in?" said Dandelion, and without waiting for an answer, he scrambled through the hole and dropped into the shoe. The old woman looked at him for a minute very angrily, then burst into tears. "'Oh, the little werches!' she cried, "'they do lead me alive. Oh dear, oh dear, oh dear, oh dear, I've got so many children. I don't know what to do.' "'How many?' asked Dandelion. "'Five, if you'll believe me,' cried the little dame, and then always getting into mischief. "'There's six of us,' said Dandelion. "'Oh, your poor maw!' cried the old woman with uplifted hands and upturned eyes. "'She likes it,' said Dandelion. "'Don't tell me,' cried the old woman. "'I know what children are. Always disobedient. By this very night, E. and D. walked through the puddle of purpose. Who did?" asked Dandelion, thinking he had not heard a right. "'E. and D. my dear,' answered the little woman. "'I call us and by the letters of the alphabet. It saves the trouble of thinking her names. I call my eldest Arabella. Ada, Alice, but when they come so quick, leters was good enough. Now, see," she broke off, "'Back to bed at once with you, or I'll whip you again. And you shamed have any broth tomorrow.' Dandelion turned quickly and saw five little tear-stained faces, peeping at him from the toe of the shoe. "'Perhaps,' he said softly. "'If he didn't whip them, they do as they were told. The old dame looked at him in astonishment. And perhaps the broth isn't so nice as what mum makes," added Dandelion. "'They couldn't cry if they had a cup of her broth. "'Dear me!" cried the old woman. "'What next?" "'You come and see my mum,' said Dandelion. "'Have a talk to her. I'll call for you when I fetch the water. "'Well, now,' said the old dame. "'It ain't often I go on a little visit. It'll be a change." And she put on her bonnets. That night the old woman woke the children up, gave them each a kiss, which surprised them very much. But they were still more astonished. And she gave them each some broth with bread. "'My dears,' she said, "'you're going to have bread in your broth every night. And I've got a new soup recipe in my pocket. What's more, you've all been asked out to tea tomorrow. So I'm going to put your hair and curl papers. "'Oh no, no!" Scream the children. "'You'll pull, then we shall cry, and you'll beat us. "'I ain't going to beat you no more,' my dears,' said the old woman, kissing them all again. "'I've learned a lesson." "'Come in my dear. Come in!' cried Mrs. Goblin. "'I've lit a fire in the dryroom so he can have a little talk while the children play in the kitchen. "'And now, my dear,' she added, when they were alone. "'Now you're here. What's to hinder you from stopping?' The old woman was crying for joy. "'But so many children!' she said presently. "'The more that may air, we'll all live together and be as happy as the night is short. And so they did. And of section 6."

For more information or to volunteer, please visit Librabox.org.
Read by Greg Giordano.
Bedtime Stories by Anonymous.
When the old woman moved from the shoe, there was once a little goblin woman who had
six sons.
They were very ugly, little boys with brown, whizzing faces, and eyes as black as
slow as they had very big heads.
Though they were so fat, their legs were so thin, you wouldn't believe.
Now the little goblin woman thought the world of her six little sons, and she had given
them all pretty far names.
Slower, butch-up, soirl, bluebell, cow slip, and dandelion.
The little goblin woman was very poor, but the way she managed was something wonderful.
She and her little family lived in a hollow tree with a little garden rounded.
There were three floors inside, and the little string ladder connected the rooms.
The first floor was a kitchen, who was ever so clean and cozy.
Then it was here that the family had meals and sat in the cold winter evenings, round
a little twig fire, listening to the wind and rain outside.
The next floor was a bedroom, and the top floor was the drawing room.
Its walls were decorated with colored leaves, and it was here that visitors were received.
Well, one cold, rainy evening, the whole family was sitting round the kitchen fire, drinking
broth.
When the little goblin woman said,
"'Dandelion, it's your turn to fetch the water tonight, dearie.'"
"'Yes, Mom,' said Dandelion, and taking his last drink of broth, he ran to wash his acorn
cup and put it away.
"'You do make lovely broth, Mom,' said Clover, smacking his lips.
"'Not half!' cried the door.
With the other five little goblins altogether.
"'That's right,' said the little goblin woman.
"'Goodbye, Dandelion, lovey, don't be gone all night.
"'Goodbye, Mom,' cried Dandelion, nearly hugging the little woman's head off, and away
he ran, with the wooden bucket over his head.
"'He had not gone far through the forest, near her to sound of crying, so he took the bucket
from over his head and looked about.
And it was, that he saw a great big shoe standing in the puddle, and it was from this
shoe that the sound of crying came.
Dandelion crept nearer, and, looking through a little hole in the heel, was just in time
to see an old woman smacking a weak girl about his own size.
"'Hey,' cried Dandelion, "'stop that!' in her astonishment.
The old woman stopped, and a little girl scuttled into the toe of the shoe.
May I come in?" said Dandelion, and without waiting for an answer, he scrambled through
the hole and dropped into the shoe.
The old woman looked at him for a minute very angrily, then burst into tears.
"'Oh, the little werches!' she cried, "'they do lead me alive.
Oh dear, oh dear, oh dear, oh dear, I've got so many children.
I don't know what to do.'
"'How many?' asked Dandelion.
"'Five, if you'll believe me,' cried the little dame, and then always getting into
mischief.
"'There's six of us,' said Dandelion.
"'Oh, your poor maw!' cried the old woman with uplifted hands and upturned eyes.
"'She likes it,' said Dandelion.
"'Don't tell me,' cried the old woman.
"'I know what children are.
Always disobedient.
By this very night, E. and D. walked through the puddle of purpose.
Who did?" asked Dandelion, thinking he had not heard a right.
"'E. and D. my dear,' answered the little woman.
"'I call us and by the letters of the alphabet.
It saves the trouble of thinking her names.
I call my eldest Arabella.
Ada, Alice, but when they come so quick, leters was good enough.
Now, see," she broke off, "'Back to bed at once with you, or I'll whip you again.
And you shamed have any broth tomorrow.'
Dandelion turned quickly and saw five little tear-stained faces, peeping at him from the
toe of the shoe.
"'Perhaps,' he said softly.
"'If he didn't whip them, they do as they were told.
The old dame looked at him in astonishment.
And perhaps the broth isn't so nice as what mum makes," added Dandelion.
"'They couldn't cry if they had a cup of her broth.
"'Dear me!" cried the old woman.
"'What next?"
"'You come and see my mum,' said Dandelion.
"'Have a talk to her.
I'll call for you when I fetch the water.
"'Well, now,' said the old dame.
"'It ain't often I go on a little visit.
It'll be a change."
And she put on her bonnets.
That night the old woman woke the children up, gave them each a kiss, which surprised
them very much.
But they were still more astonished.
And she gave them each some broth with bread.
"'My dears,' she said, "'you're going to have bread in your broth every night.
And I've got a new soup recipe in my pocket.
What's more, you've all been asked out to tea tomorrow.
So I'm going to put your hair and curl papers.
"'Oh no, no!"
Scream the children.
"'You'll pull, then we shall cry, and you'll beat us.
"'I ain't going to beat you no more,' my dears,' said the old woman, kissing them
all again.
"'I've learned a lesson."
"'Come in my dear.
Come in!' cried Mrs. Goblin.
"'I've lit a fire in the dryroom so he can have a little talk while the children play
in the kitchen.
"'And now, my dear,' she added, when they were alone.
"'Now you're here.
What's to hinder you from stopping?'
The old woman was crying for joy.
"'But so many children!' she said presently.
"'The more that may air, we'll all live together and be as happy as the night is short.
And so they did.
And of section 6."
